mnemonics.title: Directory Types Memory Aid
mnemonics.content: SINGLE TWICE TREE ACYCLIC GENERAL: Single-level, Two-level, Tree-structured, Acyclic Graph, General Graph - each increasing in complexity and capability.

mnemonics.title: Directory Operations
mnemonics.content: CRUD FOR LIST: Create, Read (List/Search), Update (Rename), Delete, Find, Open, Close, Rename - basic directory operations.

mnemonics.title: Linear vs Hash Search
mnemonics.content: LINEAR SEARCH: Low (O(n)), Inefficient. HASH SEARCH: High (O(1)), Efficient.

acronyms.usage: Top-level directory in two-level systems containing all user directories
Private directory assigned to each user in two-level systems
Data structure storing file metadata (size, permissions, timestamps, data block pointers)
Directory context for resolving relative paths
Directory implementation using linked list of cluster numbers
Structure used in acyclic graph directories allowing shared subdirectories
Unix file system metadata structure storing file attributes and block pointers

visualTips.visualization: Think of an inverted tree with root at top, branches as subdirectories, and leaves as files. Navigation is like finding a specific branch and leaf.
Imagine a library catalog with numbered drawers. Hash function tells you exactly which drawer (bucket) contains your book (file entry).
Think of a phonebook with entries in alphabetical order - you must scan through names to find your target.
Family tree diagram where multiple branches can merge upward but never form circular family relationships.
Like reading a postal address from country down to house number (absolute), or describing directions from your current location (relative).

analogies.analogy: Just as a book index maps topics to page numbers, a directory maps file names to their storage locations (inodes/FCBs).
Companies use org charts to organize employees; file systems use directory trees to organize files into departments (folders) and sub-departments (subfolders).

analogies.concept: Hash table as library call number
analogies.analogy: Library books have call numbers that immediately tell you the shelf location - hash functions work similarly for instant file access.

analogies.concept: Mount point as power outlet adapter
analogies.analogy: Just as an adapter lets you plug devices into different outlets, mount points let you attach different file systems to the directory tree.

analogies.concept: Current working directory as your current location in a building
analogies.analogy: When someone says 'go to the meeting room', you start from where you are standing - current working directory is your reference point for relative paths.
